family_history: Father alive at age 68, history of hypertension controlled with lisinopril
No family history of sudden cardiac death, arrhythmias, or syncope

social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two children in a suburban home
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 2-3 beers per week on weekends
social_history.exercise: Plays recreational soccer on weekends, jogs 2-3 times per week

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. Follows all commands appropriately. Attention intact on serial sevens. Immediate and delayed recall 3/3. No evidence of confusion or post-ictal state at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally, 3 mm to 2 mm. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Face symmetric with equal activation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No tremor or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all four extremities. No dermatomal sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady and normal base gait. Tandem gait performed without difficulty. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. No carotid bruits auscultated bilaterally.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden loss of consciousness at work without warning
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Carlos Delgado is a 40-year-old right-handed man who presents to the emergency department after an episode of sudden loss of consciousness that occurred approximately 90 minutes ago while at work. According to three coworkers who witnessed the event, Mr. Delgado was standing at his desk reviewing inventory reports when he suddenly collapsed to the ground without any preceding warning symptoms. The witnesses reported that his eyes rolled back, he became pale, and he was unresponsive for approximately 15 to 20 seconds. There were no tonic-clonic movements, no tongue biting, and no urinary incontinence during the episode. He regained consciousness spontaneously and was described as immediately lucid, able to answer questions about what he had been doing moments before the event, and oriented to his surroundings within seconds of awakening. He denied any preceding lightheadedness, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, diaphoresis, or visual changes before losing consciousness. He reports no aura, no unusual smells or tastes, and no focal weakness or numbness after the event. He has no prior history of syncope, seizures, or episodes of loss of consciousness. He denies recent illness, fever, changes in medications, excessive caffeine intake, or use of any new supplements. He had a normal breakfast this morning and was well-hydrated. He reports that over the past three months, he has occasionally noticed brief episodes where his heart felt like it was racing for a few seconds while at rest, but these were fleeting and he dismissed them as being related to stress at work. He has never sought medical attention for these sensations. His coworkers called emergency medical services, and on arrival the paramedics documented a regular pulse at 74 beats per minute, blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 99 percent on room air. He remained fully alert and oriented during transport and denied any recurrence of symptoms.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening

findings.clinical_correlation: Normal awake background, no epileptiform features

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No change in background. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No seizures captured during the 30-minute recording. The normal EEG does not support a primary epileptic etiology for the patient's episode of loss of consciousness.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute study; brief interictal epileptiform discharges may not be captured on a single routine EEG.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged EEG monitoring if clinical suspicion for seizures remains high
Correlate with cardiac workup given normal EEG findings

intervals.PR: 120 ms (short, at lower limit of normal)

findings: Short PR interval of 120 ms
Wide QRS complex with right bundle branch block morphology (rsR' pattern in V1-V2, wide S waves in leads I and V6)
Delta waves identified as slurred upstroke of the QRS complex best seen in leads II, III, aVF, and V4-V6
Posterior left hemiblock pattern with left posterior fascicular block morphology
No ST-segment elevation or depression
No T-wave inversions suggestive of ischemia
No pathological Q waves
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm with a short PR interval (120 ms), wide QRS complex (148 ms) with right bundle branch block morphology, and delta waves consistent with ventricular pre-excitation. The pattern of RBBB with posterior left hemiblock is atypical and may represent a pre-excitation pattern mimicking conduction disease. Findings are consistent with Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome. The combination of a short PR interval with delta waves and widened QRS is the classic triad of WPW and raises concern for an accessory pathway capable of mediating re-entrant tachycardia and syncope.
ecg.clinical_correlation: In the context of syncope in a young patient, this ECG pattern is highly significant. WPW syndrome with an accessory pathway can precipitate supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) or, less commonly, rapid ventricular rates during atrial fibrillation, both of which can cause hemodynamic compromise and syncope. Cardiology consultation and electrophysiology study are strongly recommended.

Cardiac.clinical_significance: Normal troponin rules out acute myocardial injury
Normal BNP argues against heart failure as a cause of syncope

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values are within normal limits. CBC is unremarkable, ruling out anemia as a cause of syncope. Basic metabolic panel is normal, ruling out electrolyte derangements and hypoglycemia. Troponin I is undetectable, excluding acute myocardial injury. BNP is normal, arguing against heart failure. TSH is normal, ruling out thyroid dysfunction. The normal laboratory profile directs attention toward a primary cardiac arrhythmia mechanism for the syncopal episode.

Echocardiogram.value: No significant valvular disease. Trivial tricuspid regurgitation.

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates normal left ventricular size and systolic function with an ejection fraction of 62%. Normal chamber dimensions. No left ventricular hypertrophy. No structural abnormalities identified. No significant valvular disease. No pericardial effusion. Normal right ventricular size and function. This structurally normal heart in the setting of WPW pattern on ECG suggests the syncope is likely arrhythmia-mediated rather than structural.

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with persistent delta waves and short PR interval throughout the recording
findings: Persistent ventricular pre-excitation (delta waves) throughout the 24-hour recording
Three episodes of narrow-complex tachycardia at rates of 178-196 bpm, lasting 8-45 seconds each, consistent with orthodromic atrioventricular re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT)
The longest SVT episode (45 seconds) was associated with the patient reporting palpitations in the diary
No episodes of atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter detected
No significant pauses (>2.0 seconds)
Heart rate range: 54-196 bpm
No ventricular ectopy of clinical significance
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitoring confirms persistent ventricular pre-excitation consistent with WPW syndrome. Three episodes of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (likely orthodromic AVRT) were captured, with rates up to 196 bpm and the longest episode lasting 45 seconds. These tachyarrhythmia episodes provide a plausible mechanism for the patient's syncopal event and reported intermittent palpitations. Electrophysiology study with catheter ablation is recommended.
output.clinical_correlation: The captured SVT episodes strongly support that the syncope was arrhythmia-mediated via the accessory pathway. The rapid rates during SVT could cause sufficient hemodynamic compromise to result in syncope, particularly if a longer or faster episode occurred.

Tilt Table Test.clinical_significance: No orthostatic hypotension or vasovagal syncope reproduced

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is negative. No hemodynamic changes suggestive of vasovagal syncope or orthostatic hypotension were reproduced during 45 minutes of head-up tilt at 70 degrees, including after isoproterenol provocation. This result argues against a neurocardiogenic mechanism and is consistent with a primary cardiac arrhythmia etiology for the syncope.

output.query: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome syncope management electrophysiology ablation

results.title: 2015 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Management of Adult Patients With Supraventricular Tachycardia
results.authors: Page RL, Joglar JA, Caldwell MA, et al.
results.summary: Catheter ablation of the accessory pathway is recommended (Class I) for patients with WPW syndrome who have experienced syncope or hemodynamically significant arrhythmias. Risk stratification with electrophysiology study is recommended to assess the antegrade refractory period of the accessory pathway.

results.title: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome: Tips and Tricks for the General Cardiologist
results.authors: Bhatia A, Sra J, Akhtar M.
results.summary: WPW syndrome can mimic ischaemic heart disease, ventricular hypertrophy, and bundle branch block on ECG. The pre-excitation pattern with a short PR interval, delta waves, and widened QRS should prompt referral for electrophysiology study. Syncope in WPW patients is a high-risk feature warranting urgent EP study and ablation.

results.title: Risk Stratification and Management of Asymptomatic and Symptomatic WPW
results.summary: Patients with symptomatic WPW, particularly those with syncope, are at increased risk for sudden cardiac death due to rapid conduction of atrial fibrillation over the accessory pathway. EP study can determine the shortest pre-excited RR interval during atrial fibrillation, which is a key risk marker. Catheter ablation has a success rate exceeding 95% with low complication rates.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend catheter ablation (Class I recommendation) for WPW patients with syncope. Syncope is considered a high-risk feature because it may indicate rapid conduction through the accessory pathway during tachycardia, with potential for hemodynamic collapse. Electrophysiology study is essential for risk stratification and localization of the accessory pathway prior to ablation. WPW can mimic other ECG patterns including bundle branch block and ischemic heart disease.

output.proposed: Procainamide IV for acute management of WPW-related tachycardia
contraindications: AV nodal blocking agents (verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, adenosine) are CONTRAINDICATED in WPW with pre-excited atrial fibrillation as they may enhance conduction through the accessory pathway and precipitate ventricular fibrillation
warnings: Procainamide may cause hypotension during IV administration; monitor blood pressure closely
QTc prolongation risk with procainamide; monitor ECG during infusion
Avoid beta-blockers in pre-excited atrial fibrillation for similar reasons as calcium channel blockers
alternatives: Ibutilide for pharmacological cardioversion if needed
Electrical cardioversion for hemodynamically unstable tachycardia
Definitive treatment: catheter ablation of accessory pathway

output.rhythm: Normal sinus rhythm transitioning to sinus tachycardia with exercise

intervals.PR: Variable, 120-160 ms (delta waves become less prominent at peak exercise)
intervals.QRS: 112-148 ms (QRS narrows with exercise as AV nodal conduction predominates)

findings: At rest: persistent delta waves and short PR interval consistent with WPW
During exercise: gradual loss of pre-excitation pattern (delta waves disappear) at heart rate of approximately 140 bpm
At peak exercise (168 bpm): QRS normalizes to 92 ms without delta waves, suggesting rate-dependent block in the accessory pathway
No ST-segment changes suggestive of ischemia
No exercise-induced arrhythmias during the test
Recovery: delta waves return as heart rate decreases below 130 bpm
Exercise capacity: 11.2 METs, normal for age and sex
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test demonstrates intermittent pre-excitation that disappears at higher heart rates, suggesting the accessory pathway has a relatively long antegrade refractory period. This finding is generally considered favorable from a risk stratification standpoint, as it suggests the pathway may not support rapid conduction during atrial fibrillation. However, this does not eliminate the need for EP study, as the pathway clearly mediates re-entrant tachycardia causing syncope. No evidence of exercise-induced ischemia.
output.clinical_correlation: Loss of pre-excitation during exercise is a somewhat reassuring finding but does not obviate the need for definitive electrophysiology study and likely ablation given the history of syncope.

signal_characteristics.Late_gadolinium_enhancement: No enhancement, no fibrosis or scar

additional_observations: No arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) features: no fatty infiltration, no regional wall motion abnormalities, no RV dilation
No hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: normal wall thickness, no dynamic outflow tract obstruction
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis or myocarditis
output.impression: Structurally normal heart on cardiac MRI. Normal biventricular size and systolic function. No late gadolinium enhancement to suggest myocardial fibrosis, scar, or infiltrative disease. No features of ARVC, HCM, or other structural cardiomyopathy. Findings support an accessory pathway-mediated arrhythmia as the cause of syncope rather than structural heart disease.
recommended_actions: Proceed with electrophysiology study for accessory pathway characterization and ablation

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome with accessory pathway-mediated tachyarrhythmia

differential.key_distinguishing: Short PR interval, delta waves, and wide QRS on ECG forming the classic WPW triad; young patient with syncope and intermittent palpitations; structurally normal heart
No tonic-clonic movements witnessed, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue bite, no incontinence, normal EEG; immediate return to lucidity after the event argues strongly against seizure
No identifiable trigger (pain, prolonged standing, emotional stress), no prodromal symptoms (lightheadedness, warmth, nausea), and negative tilt table test argue against vasovagal mechanism
Normal troponin, no ST changes on ECG apart from pre-excitation, young age without cardiac risk factors; WPW can mimic ischemic patterns on ECG

Can present with pre-excitation and conduction abnormalities similar to WPW but is familial with associated ventricular hypertrophy; no family history of similar ECG findings or conduction disease, no LVH on echocardiography

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for cardiac cause of syncope
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short PR interval, delta waves, wide QRS consistent with WPW syndrome

optimal_actions.action: Order routine blood work including CBC, BMP, troponin, and BNP to rule out metabolic and myocardial causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs ruling out metabolic, electrolyte, and myocardial injury causes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to exclude epileptic etiology for loss of consciousness
Request transthoracic echocardiogram to assess for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Structurally normal heart with normal systolic function

optimal_actions.action: Request Holter monitor to capture paroxysmal arrhythmias related to accessory pathway
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent pre-excitation with captured episodes of SVT/AVRT

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on WPW syndrome management with syncope
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Class I recommendation for catheter ablation in symptomatic WPW with syncope

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for antiarrhythmic management, particularly contraindications to AV nodal blocking agents
optimal_actions.expected_finding: AV nodal blockers contraindicated in WPW; procainamide or electrical cardioversion preferred

optimal_actions.action: Refer for electrophysiology study and catheter ablation of accessory pathway
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Definitive treatment for WPW with syncope

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG and identify the WPW pattern (short PR, delta waves, wide QRS)
Recognize that syncope in WPW is a high-risk feature requiring urgent cardiology referral
Avoid AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin) which are contraindicated in WPW
Order echocardiogram to rule out structural heart disease
Recommend electrophysiology study and catheter ablation as definitive treatment
contraindicated_actions: Administering adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, or digoxin which could precipitate ventricular fibrillation in WPW
Discharging the patient without cardiology consultation given syncope with WPW pattern
Initiating antiepileptic medications based on the single syncopal event without evidence of seizure
Attributing the syncope to vasovagal mechanism without investigating the ECG findings
key_reasoning_points: The ECG pattern of short PR interval, delta waves, and widened QRS forms the classic triad of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, which can mimic bundle branch block and ischemic heart disease
WPW syndrome involves an accessory conduction pathway that bypasses the AV node, creating a substrate for re-entrant tachycardia that can cause syncope through hemodynamic compromise
The published case demonstrated that what initially appeared to be RBBB with posterior left hemiblock was actually a pre-excitation pattern mimicking conduction disease

Syncope in WPW is a high-risk feature that warrants urgent risk stratification because rapid conduction through the accessory pathway during atrial fibrillation can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death
Normal EEG and absence of post-ictal features effectively exclude epileptic seizure as the etiology
A structurally normal heart on echocardiography in the setting of WPW points to arrhythmia-mediated syncope rather than structural cardiac disease
The absence of family history of similar ECG findings makes familial PRKAG2-related conduction disorder unlikely, as reported in the source case discussion

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic WPW triad on ECG (short PR, delta waves, wide QRS) in a young patient presenting with syncope. The ECG findings clearly point to the diagnosis with minimal confounders.
